review_summary: The transcript is clear enough to create questions about limitations of supervised fine-tuning: the cost of expert demonstrations, open-ended tasks without a single gold answer, and mismatch between token-level objectives and human preferences.

qa_outputs.qa_rationale: The item is aligned, grounded, and does not need repair after the prior cleanup pass.
The item is aligned, grounded, and does not need repair after the prior cleanup pass.
The item is aligned, grounded, and does not need repair after the prior cleanup pass.

repaired_question_bank.question: What is one major practical limitation of supervised fine-tuning?
choices: It requires many high-quality human demonstration answers, especially for specialized tasks.
It cannot be used with decoder-only models.
It cannot be applied to open-ended questions.
It always creates reward hacking during training.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: As questions become harder and more specialized, collecting correct and high-quality human demonstrations becomes more expensive. This is a major bottleneck for SFT.

transcript_quotes: Collecting expert demonstrations is expensive for complex or specialized tasks.

repaired_question_bank.question: Why can token-level loss fail to reflect answer quality well on open-ended tasks?
choices: Because it may penalize semantically mild and severe mistakes in nearly the same way.
Because it only works for image data, not text data.
Because it forces the model to generate every possible valid answer token by token at the same time.
Because it always optimizes human preferences directly before optimizing tokens.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: In open-ended tasks, not every difference is equally serious. A small semantic mismatch and a very wrong answer can receive similar token-level treatment, even though the user experience is very different.

transcript_quotes: Token-level loss can penalize semantically mild and severe mistakes equally.

repaired_question_bank.question: What is the core objective mismatch in SFT?
choices: The real goal is output that humans prefer, but SFT still optimizes next-token prediction on selected data.
SFT directly optimizes human reward, so there is no objective mismatch.
SFT optimizes generation speed and therefore ignores answer content entirely.
SFT only has problems when the model is too small; large models avoid them completely.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: This is why the lecture moves toward preference optimization. Even when the data is carefully selected, the SFT objective is still token prediction rather than directly modeling what humans prefer in the final output.

transcript_quotes: SFT still optimizes next-token prediction rather than human preference directly.

item_calibration_bootstrap.calibration_rationale: "What is one major practical limitation of supervised fine-tuning" is a direct reinforcement check and should be on the easier side.
"Why can token-level loss fail to reflect answer quality well on open-ended tasks" asks for applied understanding rather than pure recall, so a medium prior fits.
"What is the core objective mismatch in SFT" asks for applied understanding rather than pure recall, so a medium prior fits.

item_phase_map.phase_rationale: "What is one major practical limitation of supervised fine-tuning" works best as an early concept check.
"Why can token-level loss fail to reflect answer quality well on open-ended tasks" is better after the learner already has the basic vocabulary.
"What is the core objective mismatch in SFT" is better after the learner already has the basic vocabulary.
